coredata.dc:title: Turn-off fluorescence of S,N-doped carbon dots for determination of two nitro-containing drugs in dosage forms and human plasma
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: El Sharkasy, Mona E.
Tolba, Manar M.
Belal, Fathalla
Walash, Mohamed I.
Aboshabana, Rasha
coredata.dc:description: This study describes the development of an environmentally-friend optical nanosensor for the rapid spectrofluorimetric assessment of two nitro-compounds, namely nitrofurantoin and dantrolene in their dosage forms and plasma samples. A one-step synthetic technique successfully created very bright water-soluble carbon quantum dots doped with sulfur and nitrogen (S,N-CQDs). Carbon was derived from citric acid, while nitrogen and sulfur were obtained from thiosemicarbazide. The dimensions of the synthesized dots were measured using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope. FT-IR spectroscopy was used to determine which functional groups were located on their surfaces. The nanosensor's fluorescence emission peaked intensely at 415 nm after excitation at 345 nm with a quantum yield of about 0.52. The inherent fluorescence of the nanosensors gradually decreased upon addition of the studied analytes in increasing concentrations. The fluorescence reduction of nanosensor with the concentrations of the investigated drugs demonstrated linear correlation within the ranges of 0.5–8.0 μg/mL and 1.0–10.0 μg/mL with limits of detection of 0.14 μg/mL (0.59 μM) and 0.23 μg/mL (0.73 μM) for nitrofurantoin and dantrolene, respectively. The recommended method was used to determine the concentrations of the investigated drugs in their commercial capsules, with recoveries ranging from 97.90 % to 101.57 % and low percent RSD values less than 2 %. Moreover, the method was adapted for the in-vitro analysis of the two analytes in spiked human plasma samples with % recoveries from 95.20 % to 102.20 %. The mechanism of interaction between each analyte and the dots was also investigated. The selectivity of the approach for measuring analytes concentration in the presence of excipients, co-formulated medications, or co-administered pharmaceuticals was further evaluated through an interference study. The suggested method's validity was evaluated in accordance with ICH criteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrofurantoin
Dantrolene
Nitro-containing compounds
Nanosensor
Quantum dots
Plasma

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stock solutions of NFT and DTL:
Analysis of NFT and DTL in spiked plasma samples:
Optimal factors influencing interaction of NFT and DTL with S,N-CQDs:
Limit of quantitation (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD):
Assessment of NFT and DTL in their commercial capsules:
Assessment of NFT and DTL in spiked human plasma samples:

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation, Bonn, Germany for kindly donating the Spectrofluorometer (Cary Eclipse from Agilent) used in this study to one of the authors (Prof. F. Belal).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural formula of (a) Nitrofurantoin and (b) Dantrolene sodium.
Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of S,N-CQDs.
Typical HRTEM images of the S,N-CQDs.
A Co-plot showing the overlap between UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NFT and DTL (4.0 μg/mL) with and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the S,N-CQDs.
Stern–Volmer plots of the (a) NFT and (b) DTL with S,N-CQDs at different temperatures.
Application of proposed method for determination of (a) NFT and (b) DTL in spiked human plasma.
Procedure for hydrothermal synthesis and application of S,N-CQDs.
Analytical performance data for the proposed method.
Determination of the studied drugs in pure form by the proposed fluorescence sensor and reference method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values between parentheses are the tabulated values of t and F at P = 0.05 [48].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intra-day and inter-day precision data for the determination of the studied drugs by the proposed method.
Determination of NFT and DTL recoveries in commercial dosage forms by the proposed fluorescence sensor and reference method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values between parentheses are the tabulated values of t and F at P = 0.05 [48].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of the tolerance limits of possible interfering drugs.
Application of the proposed spectrofluorimetric method for determination of NFT and DTL in spiked human plasma.

head.ce:title: Turn-off fluorescence of S,N-doped carbon dots for determination of two nitro-containing drugs in dosage forms and human plasma

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516 Mansoura, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516 Mansoura, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study describes the development of an environmentally-friend optical nanosensor for the rapid spectrofluorimetric assessment of two nitro-compounds, namely nitrofurantoin and dantrolene in their dosage forms and plasma samples. A one-step synthetic technique successfully created very bright water-soluble carbon quantum dots doped with sulfur and nitrogen (S,N-CQDs). Carbon was derived from citric acid, while nitrogen and sulfur were obtained from thiosemicarbazide. The dimensions of the synthesized dots were measured using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope. FT-IR spectroscopy was used to determine which functional groups were located on their surfaces. The nanosensor's fluorescence emission peaked intensely at 415 nm after excitation at 345 nm with a quantum yield of about 0.52. The inherent fluorescence of the nanosensors gradually decreased upon addition of the studied analytes in increasing concentrations. The fluorescence reduction of nanosensor with the concentrations of the investigated drugs demonstrated linear correlation within the ranges of 0.5–8.0 μg/mL and 1.0–10.0 μg/mL with limits of detection of 0.14 μg/mL (0.59 μM) and 0.23 μg/mL (0.73 μM) for nitrofurantoin and dantrolene, respectively. The recommended method was used to determine the concentrations of the investigated drugs in their commercial capsules, with recoveries ranging from 97.90 % to 101.57 % and low percent RSD values less than 2 %. Moreover, the method was adapted for the in-vitro analysis of the two analytes in spiked human plasma samples with % recoveries from 95.20 % to 102.20 %. The mechanism of interaction between each analyte and the dots was also investigated. The selectivity of the approach for measuring analytes concentration in the presence of excipients, co-formulated medications, or co-administered pharmaceuticals was further evaluated through an interference study. The suggested method's validity was evaluated in accordance with ICH criteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Several nitro-aromatic drugs (NAD), especially those containing furan and imidazolidine rings, demonstrate wide biological activities, such as antibacterial, antifungal…etc. Among NAD, we focused our study on the determination of nitrofurantoin as the drug of choice for treatment of urinary tract infections, and dantrolene for management of malignant hyperthermia.

Nitrofurantoin (NFT, Fig. 1
a), is a nitrofuran antibacterial drug used to treat uncomplicated urinary tract infections. It acts efficiently against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [1]. It exerts its action by inhibiting the bacterial growth [2,3] as bacterial nitroreductases convert NFT into electrophilic intermediates that block citric acid cycle and the production of DNA, RNA and protein[4]. Nowadays, the utilization of NFT is receiving more attention owing to its simultaneous action on several targets, which makes it less susceptible to bacterial resistance that develops in response to commonly used antibiotics, like quinolones and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole [5]. Recently, a variety of techniques, including spectrophotometry [6] spectrofluorimetry [7] carbon nanodots [8] high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], flow injection analysis [11], square wave voltammetry [12,13] and metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [14] were used to measure NFT in its dosage forms and biological fluids.

Dantrolene sodium (DTL, Fig. 1 b) is a muscle relaxant used to treat muscular spasticity (stiffness and spasms) induced by conditions such as multiple sclerosis, cerebral palsy, spinal cord injury, and stroke. Furthermore, DTL is used in combination with some other medications to treat or prevent particular instances of high temperature (malignant hyperthermia) that may develop as a result of surgical procedures and anesthesia [15]. DTL works by blocking the release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum of skeletal muscle cells [16]. The determination of DTL has been the subject of several published articles, including spectrophotometry [17,18], spectrofluorimetry [19], HPLC [20,21], HPTLC [22], UPLC[23], and voltammetry [24,25]. Although these techniques can achieve great selectivity and sensitivity, yet, they come with a number of disadvantages, including expensive equipment [9,10,12,13,20,21,23–25], high operating costs, tedious sample pre-treatment, significant risk to the researcher and the environment, the need for specialized skills, and lengthy processing times [26].

The recommended approach is based on the fact that neither NFT nor DTL have native fluorescence due to the presence of nitro groups connected to their aromatic rings. It is well known that the nitro substituent in a heterocyclic aromatic system is generally regarded as a fluorescence quencher [27]. Because –NO2 has a high electron-withdrawing property, it may serve as an electron trap, causing a charge transfer state to arise and blocking the radiative decay pathway [28]. By-passing the prohibited electronic transitions using chemical transformation from –NO2 to other substituents, in some instances, may allow for the recovery of the emission [27]. Although the prior approach of chemical transformation is effective [19], it is laborious, time-consuming, and requires multi-steps for optimization of reactions [29]. As a result, it is crucial to develop a new approach that is rapid, sensitive, selective, and easy to use. One of such phenomena is the on–off fluorescent probe of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are fluorescent by nature but become non-fluorescent in the presence of nitro-compounds such as NFT and DTL [30].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a diameter in the range of 2 to 10 nm are recognized as a distinct category of fluorescent nanomaterials that have gained a majority of scientific interest and have been extensively used in a variety of disciplines as they are chemically stable with strong photoluminescence activities, non-toxic, biocompatible, and easily prepared from inexpensive precursor materials. Additionally, they are extremely soluble in water and may be surface modified with a range of species [30]. Because of their excellent qualities, these materials are prospective substitutes for conventional hazardous metal-based quantum dots in a variety of potential applications such as biosensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery [30,31]. There have been a number of documented methods for synthesizing fluorescent CQDs, including chemical oxidation, microwave assistance, organic carbon-based techniques, and hydrothermal synthesis [30]. The incorporation of heteroatoms into carbon nanomaterials, such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), sulfur (S), phosphorous (P), and fluorine (F) has lately garnered a great deal of interest. These materials are believed to be the most promising alternative to complement carbon in material applications owing to their intrinsic features, such as their high photoluminescence capabilities, electrical properties, surface and local chemical reactivity [32,33].

A review of the relevant published research also indicated that there were very few spectrofluorimetric techniques documented for the determination of each of NFT and DTL. For NFT, the method was based upon alkaline hydrolysis with warm sodium hydroxide then dilution with propranolol [7] while DTL was determined based on reduction with zinc powder in the presence of strong acid [19]. Although these methods are sensitive, they are tedious with certain hazards to analysts and also to the environment. Recently, there is only one approach for determination of NFT relying on its interaction with nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon nanodots [9]. This study allowed the quantification of NFT in the range of (5.0 to 90.0 μΜ) which is equivalent to (1.19 to 21.43 μg/mL) and detection limit of 1.4 μΜ (0.33 μg/mL) which is less sensitive than the proposed method which can quantify NFT down to 0.14 μg/mL (0.59 μΜ). The proposed method of S,N-CQDs is eco-friendly, straightforward, and effective for in-vitro estimation of the studied drugs in plasma samples. Additionally, it possessed sufficient selectivity for assessment of studied analytes in the presence of excipients, co-formulated or concurrently prescribed drugs.

Chemicals and reagents of analytical-grade were purchased from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. Chemicals included sodium hydroxide, phosphoric acid, acetic acid, and boric acid. The investigation was made using highly pure distilled water.
-
Pure nitrofurantoin raw material was generously supplied by Kahira Pharmaceutical and Chemical Industries Company (Cairo, Egypt).
-
Pure dantrolene sodium was graciously donated by Chemipharm Pharmaceutical Industries, Giza, Egypt.
-
Macrofuran® capsules (B. No # 2020060), containing 100 mg of NFT/capsule and are manufactured by Kahira Pharmaceutical and Chemical Industries Company, Cairo, Egypt.
-
Mepafuran® capsules (B. No # 1540820), containing 100 mg of NFT/capsule, are a product of Arab Company for Pharmaceutical and Medicinal Plants (MEPACO), 10th Ramadan City, Egypt.
-
Dantrelax® capsules (B. No # 212707A), containing 25 mg of DTL/capsule, are a product of Chemipharm Pharmaceutical Industries, Cairo, Egypt.
-
All capsules were purchased from commercial sources in the local market.
-
Plasma sample was obtained from Mansoura University Hospital, Blood Bank, Mansoura, Egypt and kept frozen at −20 °C until used.

UV spectrophotometric measurements were conducted using UV-1601 PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer from Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, USA). The equipment was set at high sensitivity (800 V), and the slit width was 5 nm. A JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) from JEOL, Tokyo, was employed at a voltage of 200 KV to evaluate the particle size and morphology of the dots. The FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet - iS10 FT-IR Spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA) equipped with a Ge/KBr beam splitter and a 4000 to 1000 cm−1 DTGS detector. The measurements were made using 32 scans with a resolution factor of 4 cm−1. pH-Meter (Consort, NVP-901, Belgium) was also used. Microsoft excel (2010) was utilized to perform statistical analysis of data and to generate figures from raw data stored as Ascii format.

Hydrothermal fabrication methods for S,N-CQDs have recently been reported in the literature [34,35]. According to these methods, 0.52 g of citric acid and 0.68 g of thiosemicarbazide were mixed and dissolved in 20 mL of double-distilled water. The resulting solution was stirred and heated at 160 °C under reflux for several hours until fluorescent dots (dark orange in color) were formed. After cooling, the prepared mixture was refrigerated at 4 °C before subsequent use. The description of the preparation method is shown in Scheme 1 .

The working solution of the S,N-CQDs was prepared by transferring 5.0 mL from the original solution and diluting it with double-distilled water in a 100 mL volumetric flask. This solution was stable in the refrigerator for 20 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stock solutions of NFT and DTL:
ce:section.ce:para: NFT and DTL standard solutions of concentration (100.0 µg/mL) were prepared separately by adding 2 mL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and completing the volume to 100 mL with ethanol in 100 mL volumetric flasks. The solutions were wrapped in aluminum foil to be protected from light as nitro-containing drugs are highly susceptible to photo-degradation even in their solid form. The obtained solutions were stable for 14 days when refrigerated at 4 °C.

A Britton Robinson buffer solution was produced by combining acetic acid, boric acid, and phosphoric acid at a concentration of 0.04 M. Using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide to adjust pH, a broad range of buffer solutions (pH 2.1–12.0) was produced.

For NFT, 0.25 mL aliquots of the working solution of S,N-CQDs were added to aliquot volumes (ranging from 0.05 to 0.8 mL) of NFT solution (100.0 μg/mL) in 10 mL measuring flasks then the mixture was completed to volume with distilled water.

For DTL, 0.25 mL aliquots of the working solution of prepared dots were added to aliquot volumes (0.1–1.0 mL) of DTL solution (100.0 μg/mL) in 10 mL volumetric flasks. Then, 2.0 mL aliquot of BRb of pH 6.0 was added to each flask, the solution was mixed well, and completed to the mark with distilled water. For both drugs, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 415 nm after being excited at 345 nm. A blank solution was prepared and measured simultaneously. Plotting the quenching values of fluorescence (ΔF = F0-F) against the final drug concentrations in μg/mL allowed for the construction of the calibration curves, from which the corresponding regression equations were derived.

Ten capsules of each of Macrofuran®, Mepafuran®, and Dantrelax® were separately emptied, homogenously mixed, and then weighed. The powder from each preparation, equivalent to 10.0 mg of either NFT or DTL, was placed into a tiny conical flask, followed by the addition of 2 mL of DMSO and mixing, 50 mL of ethanol was then added and sonicated for 30 min. The resulting solution was filtered into a 100 mL volumetric flask and completed to volume with ethanol to produce a final concentration of 100.0 μg/mL. Finally, the concentration was serially diluted to get the working concentration range. The approach previously described under “Section 2.3.5″ was employed, and the corresponding regression equation was used to calculate the nominal contents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NFT and DTL in spiked plasma samples:
ce:section.ce:para: For the precipitation of proteins and the extraction of investigated drugs from plasma contents, several precipitating agents were utilized, including acetonitrile, methanol, and ethanol. Practically, ethanol and acetonitrile totally precipitate plasma proteins, while methanol is insufficient to do so. Therefore, ethanol was selected as the green solvent as described in [36]. In this method, variable volumes of NFT and DTL working solutions were added to a series of 10 mL centrifugal tubes containing 1 mL aliquots of drug-free plasma, then vortex mixed for 30 s. Ethanol was added up to 5 mL to precipitate protein. The centrifugal tubes were vortex mixed for 30 s before centrifugation at 3600 rpm for 20 min. After transferring 1.0 mL aliquots of the supernatant to a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks, 0.25 mL aliquots of S,N-CQDs (2.0 mL of BRb pH 6.0 in the case of DTL) were added, and the remaining volume was completed with distilled water to the calibrated mark in order to obtain concentrations that fall within the method’s linearity. A blank solution was measured concurrently and the analysis was performed in accordance with “Section 2.3.5″ and. Using the appropriate regression analysis, the nominal plasma content was estimated.

Here, an easy, green, and cost-effective approach was implemented to create highly fluorescent S,N-CQDs. The selected strategy is based on treating TSC, a nitrogen and sulfur source, hydrothermally with CA, a carbon source, to generate an orange aqueous solution containing the formed S,N-CQDs [37] as illustrated in Scheme 1. The produced dots solution showed noticeable blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light, a sustained homogenous phase, and no apparent precipitation.

Different techniques, including spectrofluorimetry, UV–vis spectroscopy, FT-IR, and HRTEM, were used to characterize the synthesized S,N-CQDs. Fig. S1 displays the UV absorption spectra of both CA and TSC used in the preparation process, as well as the prepared dots. The synthesized dots were found to have a UV–vis absorption peak between 290 and 360 nm, with a high value at 310 nm. This is due to excited-state energy being trapped by the surface states, which then causes strong emission [38,39].

The fluorescence properties of the fabricated dots were also investigated, and it was found that the emission of the dots is dependent on the excitation wavelength region between 310 and 380 nm, as seen in Fig. S2 . This may be because of the wide size distribution of the dots or the presence of various groups, including C, N, S, and O that are attached to the surface of the dots and give multiple surface states with various energy levels and emissive traps [30,40]. From Fig. S2 , it was also found that the emission spectra were shifted and the intensity declined at higher excitation wavelengths. Therefore, the ideal wavelengths for both excitation and emission were concluded to be 345 nm and 415 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 .

HRTEM was used to examine the dots' size and surface morphological properties. The samples were analyzed using HRTEM at a voltage of 200 KV after being mounted on a carbon-coated Cu-grid. Fig. 3 depicts a rather narrow size range of spherical particles, with diameters ranging from 4 to 10 nm.

The prepared S,N-CQDs are soluble in water because of the hydrophilic functional groups that are located on their surfaces [30]. The FT-IR spectrum showed stretching peaks of NH/OH in the range of 3500–3100 cm−1, CO of the carboxyl group has a stretching band at about 1710 cm−1, CN vibration results in a stretching peak at about 2065 cm−1, while CC and CS stretching peaks appeared at 1625 cm−1 and 1234 cm−1, respectively. CH has a bending vibration band in the range 610–700 cm−1 [41], as illustrated in Fig. 4 .

The purpose of this study was to examine how well S,N-CQDs may function as optical sensors for the determination of the two nitroaromatic drugs. The fluorescence intensity of the dots decreased upon interaction with the investigated analytes, as illustrated in Fig. S3 .

The term “quenching” describes any photophysical process that causes a probe's total fluorescence intensity to diminish. There are several methods that might trigger fluorescence quenching, including fluorescence energy transfer, the inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, and dynamic quenching [42]. The excitation and emission spectra of the S,N-CQDs, as well as the UV-absorption spectra of quenchers, were investigated to provide insight into the possible quenching mechanism. It was observed that the UV absorption spectra of each of NFT and DTL strongly overlap with the excitation spectrum of S,N-CQDs while they partially coincide with its emission spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5 . This overlap may diminish the photoluminescence intensity as both NFT and DTL can absorb radiation, preventing it from reaching S,N-CQDs [43]. Accordingly, IFE (primary and secondary) may be responsible for the quenching process [42]. Another indication that supports this conclusion is the recognized phenomenon of the gradual red shift of the fluorescence maxima at high quencher concentrations, as shown in Fig. S3
, which is related to the greater IFE [44]. Also, quenching can happen dynamically through diffusive interactions between an excited probe and a quencher or statically through the creation of a ground-state complex between the probe and the quencher. Both types of quenching processes might be distinguished based on how they respond to temperature [42,45]. In the case of dynamic quenching, increasing temperature increases energy transfer as well as diffusion coefficients, particle mobility, and energy transfer. As a result, the quenching rate constant rises along with the temperature. On the other hand, in a static one, the quenching rate constant decreases as the temperature goes up because the stability of non-fluorescent complexes is reduced [42]. The Stern-Volmer equation (Equation
(1)
) was used to demonstrate the quenching mechanism [46].
(1)
F 0
/ F
=
1
+
K
sv
Q
F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities in presence and absence of the studied drugs,
Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant,
[Q] denotes the drug concentration.

The Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence quenching was performed between F0/F and [Q] at 293 K, 313 K, and 353 K. The curve for each of NFT and DTL was linear, and the slope (Ksv) for NFT increased with increasing temperature, inferring that the quenching process was dynamic, whereas for DTL it proceeded through a static mechanism as shown in Fig. 6 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal factors influencing interaction of NFT and DTL with S,N-CQDs:

ce:section.ce:para: Using Britton Robinson buffer, the influence of pH on the quenching value (ΔF) of fluorescence intensity of S,N-CQDs by either NFT or DTL was investigated over the pH range of 2.1–12. For NFT, there was no noticeable difference in the fluorescence intensity over the wide pH range, while it was observed that pH 6.0 was the ideal one for DTL. This may be explained by the different nucleus attached to the nitro group in NFT and DTL. In NFT, the nitro group was attached to a furan ring, but in DTL, it was attached to a benzene ring. The furan ring contains an oxygen atom with high electronegativity, it will certainly have an effect on the reactivity of the attached nitro group, rendering it more reactive compared to benzene ring. After testing volumes ranging from 0.5 mL to 3.0 mL, it was observed that 2.0 mL of the buffer (pH 6.0) had the most substantial influence on quenching the natural fluorescence of S,N-CQDs.

The effect of incubation time was also investigated. The fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded at intervals between one and 60 min. It was observed that the whole reaction required less than a minute and that a constant fluorescence signal remained for more than an hour, both of which are significant benefits of this method.
The suggested approach was verified according to ICH standards [47], demonstrating its reliability.

After the processing parameters were improved, the fluorescence emission spectra of S,N-CQDs of NFT or DTL analytes were recorded. The calibration curves were then created by plotting the fluorescence intensity difference (ΔF) against the ultimate concentration levels. The linear ranges for NFT and DTL were determined to be 0.5–8.0 μg/mL and 1.0–10.0 μg/mL, respectively, as shown in Fig. S3
. Rectilinear relationships may be represented by the following regression equations:
Δ F
(
F 0
-
F )
=
41.6
C
+

0.9998

N F T
Δ F
(

F )

36.0
C
+

r
=
0.9997
f o r
D T L

Where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of nanosensors with and without the aforementioned drugs, and C is the concentration in μg/mL. Acceptable linearity of the established approach is shown by correlation coefficients (r) close to unity, as cited in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of quantitation (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD):
ce:section.ce:para: The limit of quantitation (LOQ) was established by determining the lowest drug concentration for which quantitative measurement was possible; this concentration was revealed to correlate statistically to 10 Sa/b, while the limit of detection (LOD) was confirmed to equal 3.3 Sa/b (Sa is the standard deviation of the intercept, and b is the slope of the calibration curve). Table 1 displays the acquired LOD and LOQ values, demonstrating that the proposed approach has enough sensitivity.

The suggested method's accuracy was validated by comparing the proposed method's data in raw materials and dosage forms to the data obtained by the comparative techniques [6,18]. Table 2 shows that the tabulated and calculated values of the t-test and the variance ratio F-test were not substantially different, showing that the designated procedure was accurate and precise [48]. The intra- and inter-day precisions were evaluated using three concentrations of each of the aforementioned medications, with three replicates per concentration. In the obtained results of Table 3 , the RSD values were less than 2 %, showing that the established method was precise enough for the intended purpose.

The selectivity of the suggested procedure was further investigated, and it was found that common excipients added to capsules, such as starch, talc, lactose, and magnesium stearate, did not have any effect on the fluorescence signals obtained in this reaction, as shown in Table 4 . Paracetamol as an analgesic is frequently co-formulated with DTL to augment its pain-relieving effect; it was found that it did not interfere with the method in concentrations up to 60 μg/mL. On the other hand, NFT is generally co-administered with other medications such as vancomycin, streptomycin, doxycycline, chlortetracycline, and omeprazole. Table 5 summarizes the results of measurements of the tolerance limits of prescribed medications as the concentration that results in 2 % relative error.

The value of ΦF can be easily determined when compared to a standard fluorescent molecule with a well-established ΦF value. The absorbance and fluorescence spectra of both the sample and standard were simultaneously measured in order to minimize operational error caused by photobleaching as well as other solubility difficulties. Since quinine sulfate (QS) [35] is used as a standard fluorophore (ΦF: 0.54 at 350 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4), the ΦF value of S,N-CQDs may be easily assessed by comparing the integrated values of fluorescence of each of the sample and standard solutions excited at 345 nm with the absorbance values at the same wavelength.
Φ u
=
Φ s
×
F u
/
F s
×
(
η u
/
η s
)
2
×
A s
/
A u

In this equation, A is the absorbance reading at 345 nm, Φ is the quantum yield value, F is the integrated fluorescence value, η is the solvent refractive index (equal to 1.33 for water and 0.1 M H2SO4). Both S,N-CQDs, and QS are indicated by the u and s subscripts, respectively. The results demonstrate that S,N-CQDs has ΦF value of 0.52 (52 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of NFT and DTL in their commercial capsules:
ce:section.ce:para: NFT and DTL were effectively assayed using the described approach in a variety of locally available pharmaceutical formulations. Table 4 , displays the results of statistical tests [48], t-test and F-test, to compare the data of analysis obtained by the suggested method to the data obtained by other reported methods [6,18]. The results of the t-test and the F-test, both calculated and tabulated values, showed that the two methods were non - significantly different, demonstrating the accuracy and precision of the designated method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of NFT and DTL in spiked human plasma samples:
ce:section.ce:para:
The fundamental purpose of the suggested method is to determine the suitability of a developed method for measuring the concentration of studied drugs in a variety of biological matrices, including saliva, urine, blood, and plasma. When NFT is administered intravenously at its therapeutic level, its concentration in whole blood or plasma is typically between 1 and 5 μg/mL, while it is about 1 μg/mL in the case of oral administration [49,50]. For management of malignant hyperthermia and according to FDA recommendations, a total dose of 5 mg/kg of oral DTL in three or four divided doses every 6 h, with the last dose administered 4 h preoperatively, was needed [51]. Prophylactic plasma concentrations of DTL in humans are between 2.8 and 4.2 μg/mL. Peak DTL plasma concentrations of 4.2 μg/mL were obtained 3 h after bolus injections of 0.1 mg/kg until twitching depression plateau was reached (2.2 to 2.5 mg/kg cumulative dose) [52]. The proposed method was successfully applied to determine each of NFT and DTL in spiked human plasma samples (Fig. 7 ). The corresponding regression equations were as described below:
Δ F
(
F 0
-
F ) = 66.0 C +
6 . 88
r = 0.9997
f o r
N F T
Δ F
(
F 0
-
F ) = 50.08 C -
4.37
r =
0.9993
f o r
D T L
Where: ΔF: the difference in fluorescence intensity before and after addition of drug.
C: analyte concentration in μg/mL.
r: correlation coefficient.
Table 6 shows the percentages recoveries of the two studied drugs; the results varied from 95.20 to 102.20 %.

An innovative spectrofluorimetric approach was established for the analysis of each of NFT and DTL in their dosage forms without chemical derivatization. The method is based on the fact that the fluorescence of S,N-CQDs is quenched upon interaction with different analytes. This method involves the use of hydrothermal synthesis from two common precursors (CA and TSC) to yield a nanoscale sensor that is both highly water soluble and biocompatible, with a size of less than 10 nm. Different approaches were used to characterize the generated S,N-CQDs. The investigated drugs significantly reduced the fluorescence of the sensor with different quenching mechanisms and produced a quick and durable fluorescence response with a wide linearity range of 0.5–8.0 μg/mL 1.0–10.0 μg/mL for NFT and DTL, respectively. The developed technique was fairly sensitive, accurate, and precise. The recommended procedure was extended to quantify both drugs in spiked plasma samples, and excellent %recoveries were obtained in the range of 95.20–102.20 %.

Mona E. El Sharkasy: Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing. Manar M. Tolba: Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Fathalla Belal: Supervision, Validation. Mohamed I. Walash: Supervision, Validation. Rasha Aboshabana: Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Alexander von Humboldt Foundation, Bonn, Germany for kindly donating the Spectrofluorometer (Cary Eclipse from Agilent) used in this study to one of the authors (Prof. F. Belal).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122246.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
